---
title: "Linear maps"
date: "2026-06-03"
categories: ["mathematics", "linear-algebra", "ladr"]
execute:
    echo: true
---

{{< include ../../_macros.qmd >}}

This is an augmented summary of Sections 3.A of [Linear Algebra Done Right](https://linear.axler.net/index.html) by Sheldon Axler.

## Linear map
A function $T:V\to W$ between vector spaces is called a **linear map** if it satisfies two properties:

- It is *additive* in that it commutes with vector addition: $T(u+v)=Tu+Tv$.
- It is *homogeneous* in that it commutes with scalar multiplication: $T(\lambda v)=\lambda Tv$.

:::{#exm-}
The trivial example is the **zero map** $0$, which maps all vectors to the zero vector: $0v=0$^[Now there are three uses of the symbol "$0$", for the vector, the scalar, or the map.]. There is also the **identity map** $I$ that maps each vector to itself.
:::

:::{#exm-}
Because of how differentiation works for sums and scalar multiples of functions, the map $D\in\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R}),\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R}))$ given by

$$Dp=p'$$

is linear. Additionally, a definite integral is a linear map from $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$ to $\mathbb{R}$.
:::

:::{#exm-}
Consider an arbitrary linear map $T:\mb{F}^n\to\mb{F}^m$. Denoting the $i$th standard basis by $e_i$, there exist scalars $a_{j,i}$ such that each standard basis vector of $\mb{F}^n$ is mapped to a linear combination of the standard basis of $\mb{F}^m$ as

$$Te_i=\sum_{j=1}^ma_{j,i}e_j=(a_{1,i},\cdots,a_{m,i}).$$

Then the action of $T$ on a general vector $(x_1,\cdots,x_n)\in\mb{F}^n$ can be represented as

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x_1,\cdots,x_n)&=T(\sum_{i=1}^nx_ie_i)\\
&=\sum_{i=1}^nx_iTe_i\\
&=\sum_{i=1}^nx_i(\sum_{j=1}^ma_{j,i}e_j)\\
&=\sum_{j=1}^m(\sum_{i=1}^na_{j,i}x_i)e_j\\
&=(a_{1,1}x_1+\cdots+a_{1,n}x_n,\cdots,a_{m,1}x_1+\cdots+a_{m,n}x_n).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

#### Linear map for 1-dimensional space
For a 1-dimensional vector space $V$, any linear map $T:V\to V$ is a multiplication by some scalar.

:::{.proof}
Let $v'$ be any non-zero vector in $V$. Then $v'$ is a basis of $V$, so there is $\lambda\in\mb{F}$ such that $Tv'=\lambda v'$. For any $av'\in V$, we have

$$T(av')=aTv'=a(\lambda v')=\lambda(av'),$$

and so $Tv=\lambda v$ for all $v\in V$.
:::

#### Mapping zero
Additivity implies that all linear maps send zero vectors to zero vectors:

$$T0=T(0+0)=T0+T0\implies T0=0.$$

#### Linear independence under mapping
A corollary of this fact is that, if $Tv_1,\cdots,Tv_n$ is linearly independent, then so is $v_1,\cdots,v_n$.

:::{.proof}
If the scalars are such that $a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n=0$, then we have

$$0=T(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n)=a_1Tv_1+\cdots+a_nTv_n.$$

Therefore, $a_i=0$ by the linear independence of $Tv_1,\cdots,Tv_n$.
:::

The converse does not hold; that is, a linearly independent set of vectors may not be sent to another linearly independent set under a linear map. For example, the zero map sends any set of vectors to the linearly dependent set $\{0\}$. A less trivial cases involves the vectors

$$(1,0,0),(0,1,0),(1,1,1)$$

and the projection linear map $T:(x,y,z)\mapsto(x,y)$. So *even after* removing the zero vectors in the image, linear maps do not "preserve" linear independence.

The contrapositive of the above result is that linear maps always send linearly dependent sets of vectors to linearly dependent sets. So if $u_1,\cdots,u_n$ is linearly dependent and $w_1,\cdots,w_n$ is not, then there is no linear map that satisfies $Tu_i=w_i$.

###### Impossible maps
More generally, if $u_1,\cdots,u_n$ is linearly dependent and $W$ is not trivial, there *always* exists a set $w_1,\cdots,w_n\in W$ such that no linear maps satisfy $Tu_i=w_i$.

:::{.proof}
By linear dependence, there exist $a_i$ not all zero such that

$$a_1u_1+\cdots+a_nu_n=0.$$

So *all* linear maps $T:U\to W$ must satisfy

$$0=T(a_1u_1+\cdots+a_nu_n)=a_1Tu_1+\cdots+a_nTu_n=a_1w_1+\cdots+a_nw_n,$$

and our job is to find $w_i$s that fails this. 

The choice is quite obvious. Without loss of generality, let $a_1$ be a non-zero scalar. Let $w_1$ be a non-zero vector of $W$, and set all other $w_i$s to be the zero vector. Then the above expression reduces to 

$$0=a_1w_1.$$ 

This is impossible because both $a_1$ and $w_1$ are not zero, and so $a_1w_1$ cannot be zero.
:::

#### Mapping basis
Consider two vector spaces $U$ and $W$, where $U$ is finite-dimensional. Let $u_1,\cdots,u_n$ be a basis of $U$. Then for *any* set of vectors $w_1,\cdots,w_n$ in $W$, there exists a linear map $T$ such that $Tu_i=w_i$. 

:::{.proof}
We need to first define a map from $U$ to $W$, and then show that this map is linear. Expressing each vector of $U$ as $u=a_1u_1+\cdots+a_nu_n$, we may define $T$ as

$$Tu=T(a_1u_1+\cdots+a_nu_n)=a_1w_1+\cdots+a_nw_n.$$

This is well-defined because the decomposition of $u$ into a linear combination of $u_1,\cdots,u_n$ is unique. Clearly, $Tu_i=w_i$ is satisfied.

We have to check that $T$ is linear. Let

$$
\begin{gather}
u=a_1u_1+\cdots+a_nu_n\\
u'=a'_1u_1+\cdots+a'_nu_n
\end{gather}
$$

be an arbitrary pair of vectors in $U$. Then we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(u+u')&=T((a_1+a'_1)u_1+\cdots+(a_n+a'_n)u_n)\\
&=(a_1+a'_1)w_1+\cdots+(a_n+a'_n)w_n\\
&=a_1w_1+\cdots+a_nw_n+a'_1w_1+\cdots+a'_nw_n\\
&=Tu+Tu',
\end{aligned}
$$

which verifies that $T$ is additive. Similarly, we can show that $T$ is homogeneous.
:::

Is this $T$ the *only* linear map from $U$ to $W$ that satisfies $Tu_i=w_i$? Yes. 

:::{.proof}
Suppose that $T:U\to W$ is a linear map such that $Tu_i=w_i$. Then the action of such $T$ on any $a_1u_1+\cdots+a_nu_n\in U$ must be given by

$$T(a_1u_1+\cdots+a_nu_n)=a_1Tu_1+\cdots+a_nTu_n=a_1w_1+\cdots+a_nw_n,$$

which is exactly the linear map defined above.
:::

In summary, we have seen that a linear map is *specifiable* by its action on a basis of its domain, and *uniquely* so.

###### Extending a linear map
If $U$ is a proper subspace of $V$ and $S:U\to W$ is a linear map, we may wonder if $S$ can be extended to a linear map from $V$ to $W$. That is, can we find $T:V\to W$ such that $T$ coincides with $S$ for the vectors in $U$? (Since this is trivially true when $S$ is a zero map, let us suppose that it is non-zero.)

Our first idea might be to send all vectors in $V-U$ to the zero vector. Unfortunately, this is not a linear map.

:::{.proof}
Since $S$ is not a zero map, we can find $u\in U$ such that $Su\neq 0$. Let $v$ be a vector in $V-U$, and let us consider $u+v\in V$.

If $u+v\in U$, then we have $v\in U$, which is a contradiction. Therefore, $u+v\in V-U$ as well. 

Now, let $T$ be an extension of $S$ with $Tx=0$ for all $x\in V-U$. Then we have $T(u+v)=0$ but

$$Tu+Tv=Tu\neq0,$$

violating additivity. Therefore, $T$ is not a linear map.
:::

So what can we do? If $V$ is finite-dimensional, we may use the extension of basis to extend the linear map. As it turns out, we just had to send the component basis vectors to zero, not the whole vectors.

:::{.proof}
Let $u_1,\cdots,u_n$ be a basis of $U$, and suppose that appending $v_1,\cdots,v_m$ to the set makes it a basis of $V$. We know that specifying the images of the basis vectors completely specifies a linear map, so let us define a linear map $T$ as 

$$Tu_i=Su_i\quad\text{and}\quad Tv_i=0.$$ 

This satisfies $Tu=Su$ for all $u\in U$. 
:::

Actually, we could have chosen any vector as $Tv_i$, not just the zero vector.

## Set of linear maps
#### As vector space
Let $\mathcal{L}(U,W)$ denote the set of all linear maps from $U$ to $W$, where $U$ and $W$ are vector spaces over $\mb{F}$. Let us equip it with addition and scalar multiplication defined as

$$
\begin{gather}
(S+T)v=Sv+Tv\\
(\lambda S)v=\lambda(Sv),
\end{gather}
$$

where $\lambda\in\mb{F}$. We can easily show that $\mathcal{L}(U,W)$ is a vector space under these operations.

###### Infinite dimensions
As a vector space, we can speak of the dimension of $\mathcal{L}(U,W)$. (Let's not think too hard about what the basis looks like for now.) 

$\mathcal{L}(U,W)$ is always infinite-dimensional whenever $W$ is infinite-dimensional and $U\neq\{0\}$ is finite-dimensional.

:::{.proof}
How do we even show that a vector space is infinite-dimensional, i.e. that no finite basis exists for it? Because a basis cannot be smaller than a linearly independent set, we can construct an infinite linearly independent set $T_1,T_2,\cdots$ of linear maps in $\mathcal{L}(U,W)$, by which we mean a set where $T_1,\cdots,T_n$ is linearly independent for all $n$^[In fact, we haven't defined linear independence for an infinite set of vectors before. We cannot define it as "scalars all zero if infinite sum is zero", because we haven't defined what an infinite sum of vectors means. So here, we are really *defining* what linear independence means for such sets. Note that this also implies that every finite subset of $T_1,T_2,\cdots$ is linearly independent: to see why, consider the sequence $T_1,\cdots,T_n$ that contains all vectors of the subset.].

Let $w_1,w_2,\cdots$ be an infinite linearly independent set in $W$. (This can be constructed by choosing as $i$th element a vector outside the span of the first $i-1$ vectors.) Let $u_1,\cdots u_m$ be a basis of $U$. Then we may define a sequence of linear maps $T_i:U\to W$ as

$$
T_iu_1=w_i\quad\text{and}\quad T_iu_j=0\text{ for }j=2,3,\cdots.
$$

Now consider the subset $T_1,\cdots,T_n$, and suppose that $b_1T_1+\cdots+b_nT_n=0$. Then for any vector $u=au_1$ where $a$ is non-zero, we must have 

$$
\begin{aligned}
0&=(b_1T_1+\cdots+b_nT_n)u\\
&=b_1T_1u+\cdots+b_nT_nu\\
&=ab_1w_1+\cdots+ab_nw_n.
\end{aligned}
$$

By the linear independence of the $w_i$s, this implies that the $b_i$s are all zero. Therefore, $T_1,\cdots,T_n$ is linearly independent for any $n$, and $\mathcal{L}(U,W)$ is infinite-dimensional.
:::

#### Product of linear maps
We call the composition of linear maps as their **product**. So if $T\in\mathcal{L}(U,V)$ and $S\in\mathcal{L}(V,W)$, then their product $ST\in\mathcal{L}(U,W)$ acts as

$$(ST)u=S(Tu).$$

Like all function compositions, this product is associative. Also, we can show that the linear map product distributes over the linear map addition. 

###### Failure of commutativity
The product is clearly not commutative when different vector spaces are involved. But even for operators (map of a vector space to itself), commutativity fails if $\dim V\geq 2$.

:::{.proof}
Let $v_1$ and $v_2$ be two linearly independent vectors of $V$, i.e. non-zero vectors that are not scalar multiples of each other. Let $T:V\to V$ be any linear map that satisfies 

$$Tv_1=0\quad\text{and}\quad Tv_2=v_1,$$

and let $S:V\to V$ be any linear map that satisfies

$$Sv_1=v_2\quad\text{and}\quad Sv_2=v_1.$$

Then we have $(TS)v_1=v_1$ but $(ST)v_1=0$, so $TS\neq ST$.
:::
